speaker: interviewer
text: June 7, 1995. The survivor is Cantor Morris Lang. This is reel two. Let's continue with your story.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. What I'm trying to tell you is when he took us all out of this so-called lasarett, hospital, I still don't recall why I had such nerve to go to ask him to let me live.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And of course, a few days later, I took [INAUDIBLE]. When my brother came back from camp and he heard what happened to all that war in there and he saw me still alive, we both cried. And a few-- two weeks later, maybe three weeks later, I was called out on, on a Sunday evening. And the commandant sent for me, and he said that the [GERMAN] has asked him to bring that junge [GERMAN] which means--

speaker: interviewer
text: He let you live.

speaker: interviewee
text: --bring me that young man who I spared his life. So he brought me. And he said, well, I spared your life. He said to me, now, [GERMAN]. Then he had a few other officers with him, and I should sing. And, you know, at the time, I froze. I couldn't open my mouth.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then I said, supposingly instead to sing, I'll give you-- I'll tell you a Polish monologue. And he understood Polish because he was also a volksdeutsche. So he said, yeah? Let me hear. So I started in Polish. Can I repeat in the-- in the way? Yeah, sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SPEAKING POLISH] Now what it actually means, there was a [POLISH], a Jew, who made that monologue from a Jewish soldier in the Polish army. And when I started to tell him this monologue, he started to laugh. And he had so much--

speaker: interviewer
text: Enjoyment.

speaker: interviewee
text: --enjoyment. He said, wait a minute. You have to repeat it. He called in a few other Ukraine, and a few other officers. And I had to repeat the same thing. And then he let me go. Then I was really afraid that he's going to get after me. My luck was that about three, four days later, they changed. And they brought in a new commandant, but I forgot his name. He was a [GERMAN], and I don't know where he got away. And that was my luck. From then on, I didn't volunteer anything. I kept just like--

speaker: interviewer
text: Like everybody else.

speaker: interviewee
text: --straight human being, and that's all. In the end of 1943, around November, something like this, they started to liquidate the Budzyn camp. And they took out groups of people. And me, I was among a group, they took us to Wieliczka.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about Wieliczka.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Wieliczka is a little town. It's known for a salt mine. It's one of the biggest salt mines, and it's located not far from Krakow. They took us. There was a camp there, yeah? In Wieliczka. It was under the auspices of another camp. But in Wieliczka, I was working in a stall. I mean, they took us by, by--

speaker: interviewer
text: Train? By truck?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they took us by truck there, yeah?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: But in that camp, they took us out from the camp every morning, walked us to the salt--

speaker: interviewer
text: Salt mines.

speaker: interviewee
text: --mine. And they took us with a elevator down who knows how many-- 2,000 feet down there, yeah? And we work there I think December, January, February, March, and, and April. And for five months, I haven't seen the daylight because they took us out in the morning when it was dark--

speaker: interviewer
text: And brought you back at night.

speaker: interviewee
text: They took us home [INAUDIBLE] at nighttime. And those are short days. To till and about April, I, I didn't know how because we worked there seven days-- Sunday too.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, of course, 1944 around April, they took us away from that camp, and they brought us in May, sometimes in May, to Flossenburg. Flossenburg was very high up in Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: During the day, it was very hot. And during the night, we froze. And they kept us there I can't remember how many weeks, a short time. And about June, or beginning of July, they again took us out with a transport. Of course, over there it was a camp where we found a lot of shoes and clothes from people that got killed. And they didn't take us to work except local work to clean the barracks, to clean the streets the way-- you know, in that camp. And then around the end of June, they took us out in wagons, train. And they took us into Leitmeritz, [POLISH] And Leitmeritz, I was from July, about nine, 10 months there. What we did there is they took us out every day and we dug--

speaker: interviewer
text: Trenches?

speaker: interviewee
text: --trenches. We dug those trenches for canalization or whatever it is. There was a time that we worked and we were in the fruit trees. So, occasionally, in the end of the summer, we could get an apple, a pear, sometimes a peach. It helped us to sustain ourself because they didn't give us enough. The conditions, the, the, cleanliness there wasn't too much. And we encountered also there lice and everything. And the only one thing is that could help us is a little fruit to sustain ourselves. And this finished. And one time, I was so hungry. I couldn't stand anymore on my feet. I remember my brother used to give me a piece of his bread, and I didn't want it. I wanted him to have more. He was older than me-- nine, 10 years-- so I wanted him to have. And then I found out that-- we were-- my brother and I, we were delivering bottles of gas to the welders. And there was one lonsman of mine from Krashnik whom I knew very well. And he used to be a welder. He wasn't that at home, but he learned this. And I found out that whoever could organize a few potatoes raw, if you brought it to him, it took a few seconds. And he cooked the potatoes in the--

speaker: interviewer
text: With the blowtorch.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And he got half of it and we got. So I saw that some guys load himself up with potatoes. And I found out that it was a Czechoslovakian-- by the way, Leitmeritz is in Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, They had a private kitchen for their workers. So we used to, if someone went in there to peel potatoes, then when he loaded himself with potatoes, he took the--

speaker: interviewer
text: The skins?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. Took the-- to throw out the [NON-ENGLISH]. What do you call it? You know, like a dish that you take--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: I forgot what they call it in English. So, he used to live this-- leave this on the-- on the place where they throw out the garbage.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he left it there and went away. If someone else was lucky to grab this--

speaker: interviewer
text: Pail.

speaker: interviewer
text: This dish?

speaker: interviewee
text: --pail, this pail, I could of take it in. So I grab. I was lucky one day and got this pail because otherwise, they didn't let you in. But if you carried that pail, means that you're working there. So I got them in there. When I saw those potatoes, I got meshuga. I just was crazy. So I started to pull in my bosom, my shirt, I had a little--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tied up.

speaker: interviewee
text: --tied up a little with a string. My pants, I put them in the pants. And when I was loaded, after a few minutes, it wouldn't take me long, I took the pail with the--

speaker: interviewer
text: With the peels.

speaker: interviewee
text: --with the peels on the same place and I left it for someone else. That's the way we did it. And I figured out that if I could have 10, 20 potatoes with me, I'll be a rich man. I'll give this to my lonsman, Itzhak Kaufmann, who was--

speaker: interviewer
text: The blowtorch man.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And I'll give him and that's it. As I go out I hear a yell from a Kapo. Come here. And that was a Kapo. And he said, [GERMAN]. So I told him I work in the kitchens, so I take a few potatoes.

speaker: interviewer
text: He takes me down to a, a little cellar, and he said to me, you get 25 lashes for organizing or stealing potatoes. So I said to him, I'm so hungry. He said, hungry? 25 lashes and you're going to count them too. So I take out my potatoes, and put them on the floor there. And he said, [GERMAN], all right? I said-- you have any more? I said no, no. So he sees some, and he gives me a few lashes like this over my, you know, body.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he says now, you should pardon the expression, I had to let down my pants and he had a little stool in there. I should bend over, and he went with one of those--

speaker: interviewer
text: Whips.

speaker: interviewee
text: --whips. And he said, you count. So I say one, eins, zwei, drei, and after three, four times, I don't feel anymore, you know, because these are very painful lashes. And I said, five, six. Then I said, 17 siebzehn, achtzehn, you know it's 17--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. --18. He said, Was? Which means, what? You already at 18? Start from the beginning. Well, if I didn't receive at that time 50 lashes, was nothing. And I went out. No potatoes.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. After the 25, he said, now go back to your work. Now I hardly could walk up the few stairs from that so-called cellar or basement. And as I walk up, I remember it was snow outside. It was cold. And I felt so miserable.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I thought to myself, now he left. And I have left the potatoes. If I could grab a few back to me, if I could have a few potatoes, I would be a rich man, you know? So slowly I go down those few steps and take the few potatoes. Now, to show you what it means to be hungry. And I take them and I put them into my bosom and I go out.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I walk slowly. And I says, oy, [NON-ENGLISH] Someone else shouldn't catch me. Sure enough, a German Kapo. He said, come here. And he caught me for a second time. He said, [GERMAN].

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said a few potatoes that I worked. You're not allowed to have it. Therefore, you get 25 lashes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I say to him, [GERMAN]. I said, can I say something? He said, what? Do you have something to say? Do you have any right to say something? And he gives me one with his [NON-ENGLISH] over my head. And I feel-- The blood.

speaker: interviewee
text: Blood comes down. And I said, [GERMAN], which means, when someone is sentenced to death, couldn't before his death say something?

speaker: interviewer
text: So he said, [GERMAN], which means, so say something. So instead of me to say, I dropped down my pants. And he sees my-- you pardon me-- behind is black. And he says to me, what happened? So I told him, that I got a few potatoes, and he gave me 25 lashes-- the other Kapo. And he let me have the potatoes. Now you taking away the potatoes from me. You want me to [INAUDIBLE]. Well, you'll receive five lashes. So he dropped 20. He gave me those five lashes, which I didn't-- I didn't feel anything more. But since my blood was running down, I went down under a pump of the cold water. And I put my head under the pump, and the cold water got. And, I don't know. It stopped.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I couldn't walk. I went into a corner and I stretched out on the snow. And that was my, my help. The snow, the cold from the snow took out a little--

speaker: interviewer
text: The pain.

speaker: interviewee
text: --bit of my pain. And then my brother didn't know what happened to me. Then when I came back and he saw me, and he started to cry. And he said, don't go to organize. Whatever God will give us, years, we'll live through. Please don't do that anymore. Well, for few-- for maybe eight days, I could hardly walk. So when we walked-- but I didn't want to be left in the camp. I was afraid. So, I was always in the middle of the--

speaker: interviewer
text: The group.

speaker: interviewee
text: --group. And they helped me go there. When I came to the place where I worked, the master asked me what happened. And I said, I don't know. I was so hungry, and I, I organized a few potatoes. And I showed him what happened. So he let me take it easy for a few days. And if anyone would tell me that when you're sick and you need a doctor, you need a hospital, believe me in circumstances like this, you try to help yourself the best possible way you can. And a mere fact, I'm alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then they took us from there. I was in this camp yet till about the end of March--

speaker: interviewee
text: '45. And the end of March throughout '44, remember I came to that in July of '44.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: In March '45, they start to take us out. And we walked from Leitmeritz, which was about six or 10 miles to Theresienstadt.

speaker: interviewer
text: Theresienstadt.

speaker: interviewee
text: They walked us in, in the beginning of April to Theresienstadt.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you-- how long was this march?

speaker: interviewee
text: This march? It wouldn't take a few hours.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, a few hours.

speaker: interviewee
text: Few hours.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when we came in to Theresienstadt, we encountered in there we went to [GERMAN]

speaker: interviewer
text: They deloused you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And loused us. And they gave us some-- a shirt to change and some new clothes. And we went in there, and we lived in not anymore in those wooden barracks, but in barracks like for soldiers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Permanent barracks.

speaker: interviewee
text: Permanent barracks. And there was a bed, and they didn't take us to work. And we could walk out-- we couldn't go too far away from there because I understand that there was a typhus in that camp, and we couldn't go out too far. And then for about, it took us maybe four weeks or five weeks, and all of a sudden we felt we are free. On May the 8th in the morning, there was no Germans. And we were liberated by the Russians. And we found out later that if they wouldn't have liberate us a day before May the 8th, we would have been all killed in that camp because they prepared a place with special that you would have been shot on the top and then the body falls down in a--

speaker: interviewer
text: In a ravine.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know. What do you call it? In a deep--

speaker: interviewer
text: A ditch.

speaker: interviewee
text: --ditch. So, there was a time when we were free. We embraced the--

speaker: interviewer
text: The Russians.

speaker: interviewee
text: --the Russians with their thanks. And I think that, at that time, there was-- they called it cholks, the cholks. And I think that this was given them by the American Army. And they said we should leave them alone. They have to go out and liberate more places.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Time is short. They have to go. And I remember I spoke a little Russian. And one Russian gave me a piece of chocolate. And I remember it was a Hershey bar.

speaker: interviewer
text: He got it from the Americans.

speaker: interviewee
text: It came from Americans. You know that this probably saved my life because when I left the camp, I weighed maybe 85 pounds, 90 pounds, whatever. I don't remember how it went-- were undernourished. But this piece of chocolate curbed a little bit my appetite because those who were liberated from the camp after the war, a lot of them died from overeating, you know? The body is not used to absorb all other, you know--

speaker: interviewer
text: A huge amount of food.

speaker: interviewee
text: --huge amounts of foods and we were so hungry. And that was the end. The end of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was a mitzvah. They gave you a chocolate bar.

speaker: interviewee
text: Chocolate. That saved my life. And I found out when we were liberated in Theresienstadt, that there are women too, Jewish women left. We didn't know. But I found a girl, who became later my wife in a cellar of potatoes because the, the Russians didn't--

speaker: interviewer
text: Your life is connected with potatoes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Potatoes. The Russians didn't feed us. The Germans didn't feed us. We didn't have what to eat. So we found out that there's a big warehouse with storage of potatoes. So somebody broke up the--

speaker: interviewer
text: The lock.

speaker: interviewee
text: --and we went in just like wild. We ate the raw potatoes. We were-- And I helped a girl there to get her some potatoes, and that was my wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: That became your wife.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Then I met two girls that were liberated. And as I started to talk to them, they were from the same town, from Belzyce. They recognized me because they were first cousins to my brother-in-law, as I said, Shmiel Gradel. And I started to say, tell me what happened to my sister, Goldala. And they started to cry. And they said, you could be proud of your sister, Goldala.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about Goldala now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Goldala had one year or one and a half year old baby boy. And they wanted to grab-- the SS, they grabbed the baby from her while they made the [GERMAN] in Belzyce. And she said, you can't get my baby. Over my dead body, body will you give-- will you get my baby.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to go, and I'm young. And I am-- and she says she's 30 years old. And you-- she started to call him names. But you are absolutely worse than animals. What would you gain by it when you sh-- you want to destroy a little baby in this age. And they said, what? So they said they didn't like that. So they shot the baby in her arms. Then she spat on them. So they did her a favor and, and, and they shot her.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now every time I speak about this story, I gain, literally, more respect because not everybody could do that. That was the instinct of a mother not to give up a baby infant. And, of course, that was the end of her. When we were liberated and, of course, I became a translator with the Russians--

speaker: interviewer
text: An interpreter.

speaker: interviewee
text: An interpreter. So, after a month or so of being liberated, I found out that my wife was there left with 12 other women. 12 women were left from, from maybe 50,000 or 60,000 women throughout the years. What they--

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh huh. They were called the shedim, or the devils.

speaker: interviewer
text: The devils.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because after what they went through in Majdanek, in Auschwitz, and what they went through in Bergen-Belsen, and what they went through to burn the bodies in Trawniki-- because there was, I understand, in 1943 was one bloody Wednesday.

speaker: interviewer
text: Massacre. Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Where the three camps. One camps they finished up over 18,000 Jews in Majdanek, 18,000 Jews in Poniatowa. By the way, where my brother-in-law was taken to Poniatowa, and presumably he was killed on that day in Poniatowa and, of course, about 10,000 people in Trawniki. And they left 120 men in Trawniki. And they took a transport of 40 women to Trawniki right after-- a day or two after this bloody Wednesday. And, of course, my wife was one of those--

speaker: interviewer
text: That survived.

speaker: interviewee
text: --in that transport that survived. So there were 12 women that kept themselves together. In those 12 women, among those 12 women, was also those two girls that I remember, [? Havalen ?] and [? Simala ?]. And they were cousins to my brother-in-law. And there's where I received the news about my, my, d-- my sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. So the war ended, and you decided to go back to Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me what happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I arranged, I wanted to take my wife with me, and those two girls. And they said no. Either they all go, or none of them. So I arranged to take them all. And we legally left the camp to go back to Poland. We came back to Poland. We reached the city of Lodz, and my wife said she's getting off. Of course, at that time she wasn't my wife. She's going to go back to the place where she was born.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she was going to--

speaker: interviewer
text: See if she can find anybody.

speaker: interviewee
text: --to see if she could find anybody. And, of course, later on I found out that she came back to Elblag, where there were people, and she couldn't find anybody. And the janitor said, oh, you know, he thought she came back from the other world. And I've been back to Krashnik, to my town with my brother, and I could not find anybody. So while we went back to Krashnik, we found out that there was a girl that know-- knew, that was in camp together with my wife. And she escaped somehow. And she already married there a fellow from Krashnik, a lonsman of mine-- whom I know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we got to talk. And I told her I know a [? Parelke ?] She said, [? Parelke ?] is alive? Oy, would I like to see her. I said I would like too. Where is she? She's in Lodz. So I went back for her and took her back to Krashnik. And, of course, my older brother said, if you like each other, there is no way. I said, no, we like each other to live like sisters and brothers-- like a sister and a brother because we didn't have anybody. No one was to embrace us.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said, well, get married. I said, well, we'll think about it. And, of course, on September the 1st, 1945, just six years after the war broke out, we married. We found where there was a Rabbi came back. He wasn't a Rabbi, he was a [? shulkat. ?] But he married us there. We invited all those people who came back from camp. I don't remember how many-- 50, 60 of them. And we had-- we didn't know where, but we found a lonslady who had a bigger house from her mother and father left. So, we married there. And if I could tell you that after almost 50 years, we've went back to visit all the camps. We went back because I didn't have a place where to go to visit the graves of my parents or relatives. And my wife didn't have a place where to go to visit her parents. And she sort of figured out that they have gone to Czestochowa. And all the people in Czestochowa were taken to Treblinka.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we took our family, our daughter, and her husband and our two sons and their spouses, and we went back to Poland to visit all the camps. And if there was one trip that I undertook, I know it was very painful for us. But I felt that I walk in to a camp as a free man. And that I could walk out whenever I want.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when we came to Treblinka, my wife felt that she came to like a place of a cemetery because it was only a mountain of ashes there. And there we saw the crematoriums. And if you don't see the place, you can't envision how the camp looked. And, of course, we went back also-- she went back to Majdanik, went back to Auschwitz. On the way we visited Belzec. And we came in and we found, in Belzec, about six or nine places where the crematoriums were there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I felt that this is the place. This is the cemetery where my parents and sisters and brother were exhumed there. And, of course, with the proper prayers, and we said Kaddish there. What else can I tell you? Life has been good to us.
